   133→## 5. Data Flow: Session Lifecycle
   134→
   135→### 5.1 Session Creation (Spawn)
   136→
   137→```
   138→Developer -> POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/assign { taskId }
   139→  -> Route Handler: validate workspace access
   140→  -> Assignment Guard: validate task eligibility
   141→  -> [Local provider only] Worktree Manager: create git worktree
   142→  -> Session Lifecycle:
   143→       1. Create agent_session record in SurrealDB (status: "spawning")
   144→       2. Issue proxy token + DPoP credentials (existing flow)
   145→       3. Call sandbox_adapter.createSession({
   146→            agent: "claude",
   147→            cwd: worktreePath,
   148→            env: { ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL: proxyUrl, ... }
   149→          })
   150→       4. Configure MCP: sandbox_adapter.setMcpConfig("brain", {
   151→            type: "remote",
   152→            url: "/mcp/agent/<session-name>",
   153→            headers: { "X-Brain-Auth": agentToken }
   154→          })
   155→       5. Register persistence driver on SDK instance
   156→       6. Connect event stream -> event bridge -> SSE registry
   157→       7. Update agent_session: status "spawning"
   158→  -> Return { agentSessionId, streamId, streamUrl }
   159→```
   160→
   161→### 5.2 Multi-Turn Prompt
   162→
   163→```
   164→Developer -> POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/prompt { text }
   165→  -> Route Handler: validate workspace access
   166→  -> Session Lifecycle:
   167→       1. Lookup session in SurrealDB (not in-memory registry)
   168→       2. Validate status is promptable (spawning | active | idle)
   169→       3. Call sandbox_adapter.prompt(sessionId, [{ type: "text", text }])
   170→       4. Return 202 Accepted (prompt queued/delivered)
   171→       5. Agent processes prompt, events flow through event bridge
   172→```
   173→
   174→### 5.3 Session Restoration
   175→
   176→```
   177→[Network timeout or server restart detected]
   178→  -> SDK internally detects stale connection
   179→  -> SDK replays recent events from in-memory driver (max 50 events / 12,000 chars) as context
   180→  -> SDK calls createSession() with fresh sandbox process
   181→  -> SDK rebinds session ID to new runtime ID
   182→  -> Brain updates agent_session.external_session_id to new runtime ID
   183→  -> Event bridge reconnects to new event stream
   184→  -> SSE registry notifies client: "Session restored automatically"
   185→  Note: R1 uses SDK's InMemorySessionPersistDriver. SurrealDB persistence driver deferred to cloud providers (#187).
   186→```
   187→
   188→### 5.4 Two-Plane Governance Flow
   189→
   190→```
   191→Coding Agent in Sandbox:
   192→  |
   193→  |-- LLM API request --> Brain Proxy (/proxy/llm/anthropic)
   194→  |     -> X-Brain-Auth header for spend tracking
   195→  |     -> Forward to LLM provider with credentials
   196→  |     -> Record trace
   197→  |
   198→  |-- MCP tools/list --> Brain Dynamic MCP Endpoint (/mcp/agent/<name>)
   199→  |     -> Resolve effective toolset (can_use UNION possesses->skill_requires)
   200→  |     -> Return filtered tool list
   201→  |
   202→  |-- MCP tools/call --> Brain Dynamic MCP Endpoint (/mcp/agent/<name>)
   203→  |     -> Evaluate policy graph
   204→  |     -> Inject OAuth credentials from credential broker
   205→  |     -> Forward to upstream MCP server
   206→  |     -> Record trace with policy evaluation result
   207→  |     -> Return tool result
   208→  |
   209→  |-- Native tools (file, bash, git) --> Execute locally in sandbox
   210→       -> No governance (passthrough)
   211→       -> Permission handler may intercept dangerous operations
   212→```
   213→
   214→## 6. Integration Patterns
   215→
   216→### 6.1 SandboxAgent SDK Integration (HTTP/SSE)
   217→
   218→Brain communicates with SandboxAgent Server via HTTP API (REST + SSE). The SDK client library wraps these calls. Brain wraps the SDK behind an adapter interface (see component-boundaries.md).
   219→
   220→- **Protocol**: HTTP/SSE (not WebSocket)
   221→- **Authentication**: Session-scoped tokens
   222→- **Event delivery**: SSE from SandboxAgent Server, consumed by adapter, forwarded through event bridge
   223→- **Error handling**: Fail fast on connection errors; retry with backoff on transient failures
   224→
   225→### 6.2 Dynamic MCP Endpoint (MCP Protocol over HTTP)
   226→
   227→The coding agent inside the sandbox connects to Brain's `/mcp/agent/<name>` endpoint as a remote MCP server. This uses the standard MCP protocol (SSE transport) that Claude Code and other agents natively speak.
   228→
   229→- **Protocol**: MCP over SSE transport
   230→- **Authentication**: `X-Brain-Auth` header with per-session agent token
   231→- **Tool filtering**: `tools/list` returns only the agent's effective toolset
   232→- **Policy enforcement**: `tools/call` evaluates policy graph before forwarding
   233→- **Credential injection**: OAuth tokens injected by credential broker per tool call
   234→
   235→### 6.3 Session Persistence
   236→
   237→**R1 (local provider):** Session lifecycle state (`agent_session` table) is persisted in SurrealDB. Event persistence for SDK replay uses the built-in `InMemorySessionPersistDriver` — sufficient because Brain and agent processes share the same host lifecycle.
   238→
   239→**Deferred (cloud providers):** A custom SurrealDB `SessionPersistDriver` with `sandbox_event` table and 100ms write buffering (ADR-077) is needed when sandboxes outlive Brain restarts. Tracked in [#187](https://github.com/marcus-sa/brain/issues/187).
   240→
   241→## 7. External Integrations
   242→
   243→| Integration | Type | Contract Test Annotation |
   244→|------------|------|-------------------------|
   245→| SandboxAgent Server | HTTP/SSE API (localhost) | Contract tests recommended for SandboxAgent HTTP API -- consumer-driven contracts (e.g., Pact-JS) to detect breaking changes in 0.x SDK releases |
   246→| Upstream MCP Servers | MCP Protocol (various) | Already annotated in existing tool registry design |
   247→| LLM Providers | HTTP API (via proxy) | Already covered by existing proxy infrastructure |
   248→
   249→## 8. Architecture Enforcement
   250→
   251→| Rule | Enforcement Tool | Description |
   252→|------|-----------------|-------------|
   253→| Adapter interface boundary | dependency-cruiser | Orchestrator modules must not import SandboxAgent SDK directly; only through adapter |
   254→| No module-level mutable state | ESLint custom rule | Ban `let` declarations at module scope in orchestrator/ |
   255→| Dependency direction | dependency-cruiser | event-bridge, permission-handler must not depend on session-lifecycle |
   256→| Port/adapter compliance | TypeScript strict types | Adapter interface defined as type; implementation injectable |
   257→
   258→## 9. Handoff Notes
   259→
   260→### For Platform Architect (DEVOPS wave)
   261→
   262→- **Development paradigm**: Functional (types-first, composition pipelines, pure core / effect shell)
   263→- **SandboxAgent Server deployment**: Rust binary must be available alongside Brain server. Local dev: direct binary. Production: containerized (Docker image from Rivet, or custom build).
   264→- **Port requirements**: SandboxAgent Server listens on port 4100 (configurable). Brain must reach it at `http://localhost:4100` (local) or via Docker network.
   265→
   266→**External Integrations Requiring Contract Tests:**
   267→- SandboxAgent Server (HTTP/SSE API): session lifecycle, event streaming, permission handling. Recommended: consumer-driven contracts via Pact-JS in CI acceptance stage to detect breaking changes in 0.x releases before production.
   268→
   269→### For Acceptance Designer (DISTILL wave)
   270→
   271→- All acceptance criteria from DISCUSS wave user stories are preserved unchanged
   272→- New event types (`agent_permission_request`, `agent_permission_response`, `agent_restoration`) need SSE stream assertions in acceptance tests
   273→- Session persistence requires SurrealDB migration for `agent_session` sandbox fields (`provider`, `session_type`)
   274→- Multi-turn prompt flow replaces the current 409 rejection -- this is the core behavioral change
   275→- Mock adapter injection point: `SandboxAgentAdapter` type in `sandbox-adapter.ts`
   276→

1→# Research: SandboxAgent SDK for Brain Agent Execution
     2→
     3→**Date**: 2026-03-25
     4→**Research Question**: Would SandboxAgent SDK be ideal for agent execution in Brain, particularly for governed tool access via MCP config and skills injection via skills config?
     5→**Prior Art**: `docs/research/brain-native-agent-runtime.md` (concluded Brain should run agents natively)
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## 1. What SandboxAgent Is
    10→
    11→SandboxAgent ([sandboxagent.dev](https://sandboxagent.dev)) is an open-source (Apache 2.0) Rust binary + TypeScript SDK by Rivet that provides a universal HTTP/SSE interface for controlling coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Amp, Cursor, Pi) running in isolated sandboxes.
    12→
    13→**Core value proposition**: One HTTP API to control any coding agent, regardless of their proprietary interfaces.
    14→
    15→**Execution model**:
    16→```
    17→Your Backend → SDK (HTTP) → Sandbox Agent Server (Rust) → Agent Process (Claude Code, etc.)
    18→                                  ↕
    19→                           Sandbox (E2B / Docker / Daytona / Vercel)
    20→```
    21→
    22→**Key capabilities**:
    23→- Session lifecycle management (create, prompt, stream events, resume, destroy)
    24→- Universal event schema across all supported agents
    25→- Permission request handling (approve/reject tool use)
    26→- MCP server configuration per session
    27→- Skills configuration per session
    28→- File system, process, and terminal access within sandbox
    29→- Inspector UI for debugging
    30→- OpenTelemetry observability
    31→
    32→Sources: [GitHub](https://github.com/rivet-dev/sandbox-agent), [Docs](https://sandboxagent.dev/docs)
    33→
    34→---
    35→
    36→## 2. MCP Configuration Analysis
    37→
    38→SandboxAgent supports configuring MCP servers that agents can use within sessions.
    39→
    40→### What it provides
    41→
    42→```typescript
    43→// Set MCP config for a working directory
    44→await sdk.setMcpConfig("/workspace", "my-server", {
    45→  type: "local",  // or "remote"
    46→  command: "/path/to/server",
    47→  args: ["--flag"],
    48→  env: { API_KEY: "..." },
    49→  enabled: true,
    50→  timeout: 30000,
    51→});
    52→
    53→// Remote servers support bearer tokens
    54→await sdk.setMcpConfig("/workspace", "remote-server", {
    55→  type: "remote",
    56→  url: "https://api.example.com/mcp",
    57→  transport: "sse",
    58→  headers: { Authorization: "Bearer ${ENV_VAR}" },
    59→});
    60→
    61→// Session inherits MCP config from its working directory
    62→await sdk.createSession({ agent: "claude", cwd: "/workspace" });
    63→```
    64→
    65→### What it does NOT provide
    66→
    67→| Brain Need | SandboxAgent Support |
    68→|-----------|---------------------|
    69→| **Per-tool filtering** (expose only granted tools from an MCP server) | Not supported — entire MCP servers are enabled/disabled, not individual tools |
    70→| **Dynamic tool injection** based on workspace grants or policy | Not supported — MCP config is static per working directory |
    71→| **Credential brokerage** (OAuth token injection, refresh, rotation) | Basic only — static env vars and bearer tokens, no OAuth flow management |
    72→| **Policy evaluation** before tool execution | Not supported — permission handling is binary approve/reject at the agent level |
    73→| **Tool governance audit trail** | Not supported — events stream tool calls but no policy evaluation records |
    74→
    75→**Critical gap**: Brain's tool registry (#178) requires resolving an agent's effective toolset as `can_use ∪ (possesses → skill_requires)` — the union of direct grants and skill-derived tools. SandboxAgent has no concept of tool-level access control. It exposes entire MCP servers or nothing.
    76→
    77→Source: [MCP Config Docs](https://sandboxagent.dev/docs/mcp-config)
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→## 3. Skills Configuration Analysis
    82→
    83→### What it provides
    84→
    85→Skills are `SKILL.md` instruction files that get loaded into agent context. SandboxAgent supports three source types:
    86→
    87→| Source | Format | Example |
    88→|--------|--------|---------|
    89→| `github` | `owner/repo` | `rivet-dev/skills` |
    90→| `local` | Filesystem path | `/workspace/.skills/` |
    91→| `git` | Clone URL | `https://git.example.com/skills.git` |
    92→
    93→```typescript
    94→// Register skill source
    95→await sdk.setSkillsConfig("/workspace", "my-skills", {
    96→  source: { type: "github", owner: "rivet-dev", repo: "skills" },
    97→  skills: ["sandbox-agent"],  // optional: filter to specific skills
    98→  ref: "main",
    99→});
   100→```
   101→
   102→### What it does NOT provide
   103→
   104→| Brain Need (Issue #177) | SandboxAgent Support |
   105→|------------------------|---------------------|
   106→| **Graph-native skills** (SurrealDB `skill` table with lifecycle) | Not supported — skills are filesystem files, not database records |
   107→| **BM25 trigger matching** (activate skills based on intent) | Not supported — all configured skills are available, no selective activation |
   108→| **`skill_requires` edges** (skills bundle tool access) | Not supported — skills and MCP tools are independent configs |
   109→| **LLM-driven tool requirement analysis** | Not supported |
   110→| **Version chains** (`skill_supersedes` relation) | Not supported — uses git refs for versioning |
   111→| **Policy governance** (`governs_skill` relation) | Not supported |
   112→| **`possesses` relation** (agent-skill assignment) | Not supported — skills are directory-scoped, not identity-scoped |
   113→| **Missing tool resolution** (gap detection + MCP server catalog) | Not supported |
   114→
   115→**Key insight**: SandboxAgent's skills are static instruction files loaded from repositories. Brain's skills (#177) are graph-native entities with lifecycle management, trigger-based activation, tool bundling, policy governance, and LLM-driven analysis. These are fundamentally different concepts that share the name "skill."
   116→
   117→Source: [Skills Config Docs](https://sandboxagent.dev/docs/skills-config)
   118→
   119→---
   120→
   121→## 4. Architecture Fit Assessment
   122→
   123→### What SandboxAgent solves well
   124→
   125→1. **Sandbox isolation** — Brain needs agents to execute in isolated environments. SandboxAgent abstracts sandbox providers (E2B, Docker, Daytona, Vercel, Cloudflare) behind a single API.
   126→
   127→2. **Agent-agnostic interface** — Brain's orchestrator could swap between Claude Code, Codex, and others without rewriting integration code.
   128→
   129→3. **Session management** — Full session lifecycle over HTTP: `createSession()`, `prompt()`, `resumeSession()`, `destroySession()`. Sessions support multi-turn interaction (unlike Claude Agent SDK's single-query model). The API includes listing sessions, fetching event history with pagination, and runtime config changes (model, mode, thought level).
   130→
   131→   ```typescript
   132→   const session = await sdk.createSession({ agent: "claude", cwd: "/" });
   133→   const response = await session.prompt([{ type: "text", text: "implement rate limiting" }]);
   134→   // Later: send follow-up without respawning
   135→   await session.prompt([{ type: "text", text: "add tests for the rate limiter" }]);
   136→   // Resume after disconnect
   137→   const restored = await sdk.resumeSession(session.id);
   138→   ```
   139→
   140→   This directly unblocks the orchestrator's reject-and-retry flow (`POST .../prompt` currently returns 409 because Claude Agent SDK doesn't support follow-up prompts).
   141→
   142→4. **Session restoration** — Automatic recovery from connection loss. When `prompt()` or `resumeSession()` encounters a stale connection, the SDK transparently recreates a fresh session, rebinds the local session ID to the new runtime ID, and replays recent persisted events as context. Configurable via `replayMaxEvents` (default 50) and `replayMaxChars` (default 12,000). This eliminates the need for Brain's orchestrator to handle reconnection logic or agent respawning on transient failures.
   143→
   144→5. **Event streaming** — Universal event schema for tool calls, file edits, and permission requests. Brain could ingest these into its trace graph. Supports both real-time streaming (`session.onEvent()`) and historical pagination (`sdk.getEvents({ sessionId, limit })`).
   145→
   146→6. **Permission handling** — Granular permission request/response model: `session.onPermissionRequest()` + `session.respondPermission(id, "once" | "always" | "reject")`. Brain's orchestrator could map this to its intent authorization system — auto-approve tools within granted scope, escalate others to the user.
   147→
   148→7. **Custom session persistence** — The `SessionPersistDriver` interface (5 methods: `getSession`, `listSessions`, `updateSession`, `listEvents`, `insertEvent`) allows Brain to write a SurrealDB driver, storing session records and events directly in the knowledge graph alongside `agent_session` and trace data. Built-in drivers exist for Postgres, SQLite, IndexedDB, and Rivet, but any backend works. Session restoration (point 4) depends on this driver for event replay.
   149→
   150→5. **Credential extraction** — `sandbox-agent credentials extract-env` pulls API keys from local agent configs, useful for sandbox provisioning.
   151→
   152→6. **Personal subscription support** — SandboxAgent can detect and use OAuth tokens from users' existing Claude Code / Codex personal subscriptions. Agents run under the user's own auth — no API key management needed for individual users. This is useful for Brain's self-hosted / personal use case where users bring their own subscriptions.
   153→
   154→### LLM Credential Models
   155→
   156→SandboxAgent supports three credential strategies, relevant to Brain's multi-tenant architecture:
   157→
   158→| Model | Mechanism | Brain Relevance |
   159→|-------|-----------|-----------------|
   160→| **Personal subscription** | Auto-detects OAuth tokens from local Claude Code / Codex login. Short-lived tokens, no key management. | Individual users running Brain locally — zero config, agents use the user's own subscription |
   161→| **API keys** | Pass `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` via `spawn.env` | Brain-managed agents where the workspace provides model access |
   162→| **Per-tenant gateway** | Scoped API keys per tenant with independent spend tracking and budget limits | Aligns with Brain's proxy spend tracking and per-workspace budget limits |
   163→
   164→The per-tenant gateway model maps directly to Brain's existing spend tracking architecture (`proxy_spend_cache` + budget limits). Brain could issue scoped keys per workspace/agent and pass them to SandboxAgent, maintaining spend isolation without exposing organizational API keys.
   165→
   166→**Credentials and governance are orthogonal.** All LLM requests from coding agents still flow through Brain's proxy for tool filtering, policy evaluation, spend tracking, and trace recording. But the LLM credentials (who pays for inference) can come from any of the three models above. A personal subscription user's OAuth token passes *through* the proxy to the LLM provider — Brain governs the request but the user's own subscription covers the cost. This means Brain doesn't need to provide LLM API keys for self-hosted users while still maintaining full governance.
   167→
   168→Source: [LLM Credentials Docs](https://sandboxagent.dev/docs/llm-credentials)
   169→
   170→### What SandboxAgent cannot solve
   171→
   172→1. **Governed tool access** — Brain's core differentiator is policy-controlled tool access. SandboxAgent exposes entire MCP servers or nothing. Brain needs per-tool filtering based on `can_use` grants, `possesses → skill_requires` derivation, and policy evaluation. This must be implemented in Brain's proxy layer regardless.
   173→
   174→2. **Tool credential brokerage** — Brain's OAuth credential management (#178) for integration tools (GitHub, Slack, Jira MCP servers) requires dynamic token injection, refresh flows, and per-identity credential isolation. SandboxAgent only supports static env vars and bearer tokens for MCP server auth. Note: LLM provider credentials are handled separately (see above) and are less of a gap.
   175→
   176→3. **Graph integration** — Brain's knowledge graph (decisions, observations, tasks, learnings) must be injected into agent context. SandboxAgent has no mechanism for dynamic context injection beyond skills files and MCP servers.
   177→
   178→4. **Skills as graph entities** — Issue #177's design is fundamentally about graph-native skills with trigger matching, tool bundling, version chains, and policy governance. SandboxAgent's file-based skills don't map to this.
   179→
   180→5. **Intent authorization** — Brain's intent system (RAR tokens, DPoP, policy graph evaluation) operates before tool execution. SandboxAgent's permission model is a simple approve/reject callback.
   181→
   182→---
   183→
   184→## 5. Integration Architecture (If Adopted)
   185→
   186→If Brain were to use SandboxAgent, it would serve as the **sandbox execution layer** for all externally-executed agents (coding agents, user-configured custom agents) while Brain's proxy and dynamic MCP endpoint form the **governance control plane**:
   187→
   188→```
   189→Agent (Claude Code / Codex / custom agent in sandbox)
   190→  │
   191→  ├── LLM API requests → Brain Proxy → spend tracking, credential forwarding → LLM Provider
   192→  │                         ↑                                                     ↑
   193→  │                   LLM traffic plane                                    user's own credentials
   194→  │                   (passthrough for tool calls)                         (personal sub / API key / gateway)
   195→  │
   196→  ├── MCP tool calls → Brain /mcp/agent/<name> → policy, credentials, upstream MCP → tool results
   197→  │                         ↑
   198→  │                   tool governance plane
   199→  │                   (per-agent filtered tools/list, governed tools/call)
   200→  │
   201→  └── Native tools (file, bash, git) → execute locally in sandbox (ungoverned passthrough)
   202→
   203→Brain Backend
   204→  ├── Proxy (LLM traffic plane)
   205→  │     ├── LLM credential forwarding (personal sub / API key / gateway)
   206→  │     ├── Spend tracking (per-workspace budget limits)
   207→  │     └── Trace recording (LLM request/response audit)
   208→  │
   209→  ├── Dynamic MCP Endpoint /mcp/agent/<name> (tool governance plane)
   210→  │     ├── tools/list → filtered by agent grants (can_use ∪ possesses→skill_requires)
   211→  │     ├── tools/call → policy evaluation → credential brokerage → upstream MCP
   212→  │     └── Trace recording (tool call audit)
   213→  │
   214→  ├── Knowledge Graph (context injection via MCP)
   215→  ├── Skill Resolver (BM25 trigger matching → SKILL.md delivery)
   216→  │
   217→  └── SandboxAgent SDK ← execution layer
   218→        ├── Session lifecycle (create, resume, destroy)
   219→        ├── Agent process management (Claude Code, Codex, etc.)
   220→        ├── Sandbox provider abstraction (E2B, Docker, Daytona)
   221→        ├── Event streaming → Brain trace graph
   222→        └── Session persistence → SurrealDB (custom driver)
   223→```
   224→
   225→### Why two planes instead of one
   226→
   227→The proxy and MCP endpoint are separate because they solve different problems:
   228→
   229→**LLM traffic (proxy):** The coding agent sends LLM API requests through Brain's proxy URL. The proxy forwards to the LLM provider with the user's credentials, tracks spend, and records traces. The proxy does **not** execute tool calls for coding agents — it is a passthrough for the LLM conversation. Native tools (file read/write, bash, git) execute locally in the sandbox without governance.
   230→
   231→**MCP tool calls (dynamic endpoint):** SandboxAgent configures Brain's `/mcp/agent/<name>` as an MCP server via `setMcpConfig()`. The coding agent discovers governed tools via standard `tools/list` (filtered by agent grants) and executes them via `tools/call` (policy evaluation + credential brokerage + upstream forwarding). This uses the standard MCP protocol that Claude Code, Codex, etc. already speak natively.
   232→
   233→**Why not have the proxy execute MCP tools?** The proxy would need to run a partial agent loop — intercept LLM responses, split tool calls into MCP (execute) vs native (pass through), feed MCP results back to the LLM, and only return to the coding agent when native-only calls remain. This creates edge cases when the LLM returns mixed MCP + native tool calls in one response (the proxy can't execute MCP tools while waiting on native results it doesn't have). The dynamic MCP endpoint avoids this entirely — the coding agent routes tool calls to the right destination naturally via standard MCP protocol.
   234→
   235→This differs from **Brain-native agents** (chat, PM, observer) where the proxy owns the full agent loop and executes ALL tools. Sandbox-executed agents (coding agents, user-configured custom agents) own their own loop; Brain governs only what it needs to.
   236→
   237→Skills config delivers activated skills as `SKILL.md` files in the sandbox.
   238→
   239→---
   240→
   241→## 6. Comparison: SandboxAgent vs Brain Native Runtime
   242→
   243→| Dimension | SandboxAgent | Brain Native (current path) |
   244→|-----------|-------------|---------------------------|
   245→| **Agent loop** | Delegates to coding agents (Claude Code, etc.) | Claude Agent SDK `query()` → local Claude Code process |
   246→| **Tool execution** | Native tools: local in sandbox (passthrough). MCP tools: Brain dynamic endpoint (governed) | Native tools: local in worktree. MCP tools: Brain CLI stdio MCP server |
   247→| **Tool governance** | MCP tools governed via `/mcp/agent/<name>`. Native tools ungoverned | MCP tools governed via Brain CLI + proxy. Native tools ungoverned (bypassPermissions) |
   248→| **LLM credentials** | Personal subscription OAuth, API keys, per-tenant gateway | OpenRouter / Ollama via config |
   249→| **Tool credentials** | Static env vars / bearer tokens | OAuth brokerage with refresh |
   250→| **Skills** | File-based, static | Graph-native, trigger-activated, tool-bundled |
   251→| **Context injection** | Skills files + MCP servers | Graph context, learnings, skills, tools |
   252→| **Sandbox isolation** | Provider-dependent: local = none (host process), Docker = container, E2B = VM | Git worktrees for local isolation (existing) |
   253→| **Agent variety** | 6 agents supported | Single agent per orchestrator config |
   254→| **Session model** | Multi-turn (`prompt()` + `resumeSession()` + auto-restoration) | Single-query (`query()` returns `AsyncIterable`, no follow-ups) |
   255→| **Session persistence** | Custom driver interface — implement `SessionPersistDriver` (5 methods) for any backend | In-memory handle registry (`Map<id, AgentHandle>`) |
   256→| **Permission model** | Granular per-request (`onPermissionRequest` → `once`/`always`/`reject`) | Binary (`bypassPermissions: true`) |
   257→| **Observability** | OpenTelemetry | OpenTelemetry (existing) |
   258→
   259→---
   260→
   261→## 7. Trade-offs
   262→
   263→### Gains from adopting SandboxAgent
   264→
   265→1. **Sandbox abstraction** — No need to build Docker/E2B/Daytona integration from scratch
   266→2. **Agent portability** — Swap coding agents without rewriting integration
   267→3. **Session management** — Battle-tested session lifecycle over HTTP
   268→4. **Inspector UI** — Free debugging/introspection tool
   269→5. **Community momentum** — 1.2k GitHub stars, active development, multiple sandbox providers
   270→
   271→### Losses from adopting SandboxAgent
   272→
   273→1. **Governance gap** — Brain's entire value proposition (governed autonomy) must still be built on top. SandboxAgent provides no tool-level access control, policy evaluation, or credential brokerage.
   274→2. **Two governance models** — Brain-native agents use proxy-executed tool loops. Coding agents use dynamic MCP endpoints with native tool passthrough. Same governance logic, different integration patterns to maintain.
   275→3. **Skills impedance** — Two different "skills" concepts (file-based vs graph-native) create confusion and require a translation layer.
   276→4. **Dependency risk** — Coupling to a 0.3.x/0.4.x SDK from a startup (Rivet) for a core execution path.
   277→
   278→---
   279→
   280→## 8. Recommendation
   281→
   282→**Use SandboxAgent as the sandbox execution layer, not as the tool governance layer.**
   283→
   284→SandboxAgent solves a real problem Brain has — it needs to run coding agents in isolated environments without building sandbox provider integrations from scratch. But SandboxAgent's MCP config and skills config are **transport mechanisms**, not governance mechanisms.
   285→
   286→### Concrete integration pattern
   287→
   288→1. **Brain resolves grants** — When spawning an agent session, Brain resolves the agent's effective toolset (`can_use ∪ possesses→skill_requires`), evaluates policies, and prepares credentials.
   289→
   290→2. **Brain exposes dynamic MCP endpoint** — Brain registers `/mcp/agent/<name>` which serves only the tools this agent is granted. `tools/list` returns the filtered toolset. `tools/call` evaluates policy, injects credentials, and forwards to upstream MCP servers.
   291→
   292→3. **SandboxAgent configures MCP** — Brain uses `sdk.setMcpConfig()` to point the coding agent at its dynamic MCP endpoint:
   293→   ```typescript
   294→   await sdk.setMcpConfig("/workspace", "brain", {
   295→     type: "remote",
   296→     url: `${brainUrl}/mcp/agent/${agentName}`,
   297→     transport: "sse",
   298→     headers: { "X-Brain-Auth": agentToken },
   299→   });
   300→   ```
   301→
   302→4. **Brain configures proxy** — The coding agent's LLM endpoint is set to Brain's proxy URL for spend tracking and credential forwarding.
   303→
   304→5. **Brain generates skills config** — Brain renders activated skills (from BM25 trigger matching) as `SKILL.md` files in the sandbox filesystem, or serves them via a local MCP server with a `get_skill_content` tool.
   305→
   306→6. **SandboxAgent executes** — The coding agent runs in the sandbox with:
   307→   - Brain's proxy as its LLM endpoint (spend tracking, credential forwarding)
   308→   - Brain's dynamic MCP endpoint for governed tools (policy, credentials, upstream forwarding)
   309→   - Native tools executing locally in the sandbox (ungoverned passthrough)
   310→
   311→7. **Brain records traces** — Event stream from SandboxAgent feeds into Brain's trace graph. MCP tool calls are also traced at the endpoint level, giving dual observability.
   312→
   313→### What this means for #177
   314→
   315→SandboxAgent's skills-config does **not** replace the need to build issue #177. Brain's graph-native skills (trigger matching, tool bundling, version chains, policy governance) are a superset of what SandboxAgent provides. However, SandboxAgent's skills-config provides a convenient **delivery mechanism** — Brain resolves which skills to activate, then uses SandboxAgent's config to make them available in the sandbox.
   316→
   317→### What this means for MCP tool access
   318→
   319→SandboxAgent's MCP config does **not** replace Brain's tool registry (#178) or tool governance. Brain must still resolve per-agent tool grants, broker credentials, and evaluate policies. SandboxAgent's MCP config is the **transport mechanism** — it points the coding agent at Brain's dynamic MCP endpoint (`/mcp/agent/<name>`) where governance happens. The endpoint is a thin layer on top of the same grant resolution and policy evaluation that Brain-native agents already use.
   320→
   321→The key architectural insight: **one governance implementation, two integration patterns**. Brain-native agents (chat, PM, observer) get governed tools via the proxy's full agent loop. Sandbox-executed agents (coding agents, user-configured custom agents) get governed tools via the dynamic MCP endpoint. Both resolve grants from the same `can_use ∪ possesses→skill_requires` logic and evaluate the same policy graph.
   322→
   323→### Orchestrator refactoring
   324→
   325→The orchestrator currently uses `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` (`query()`) to spawn Claude Code processes locally with git worktrees. Adopting SandboxAgent replaces this SDK for all sandbox-executed agents — both built-in coding agents and user-configured custom agents.
   326→
   327→**Current architecture (Claude Agent SDK — coding agents only):**
   328→
   329→| Component | Current Implementation |
   330→|-----------|----------------------|
   331→| Agent spawn | `query()` from `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` → local Claude Code process |
   332→| Agent options | `buildAgentOptions()` → `cwd`, `maxTurns`, `allowedTools`, `systemPrompt`, `permissionMode` |
   333→| MCP server | Brain CLI as stdio MCP server (`mcpServers.brain`) within agent process |
   334→| LLM proxy | `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` → Brain proxy `/proxy/llm/anthropic` with `X-Brain-Auth` |
   335→| Isolation | Git worktree per session (`agent/<task-slug>-<uuid>` branch) |
   336→| Events | `AsyncIterable<unknown>` → event bridge → SSE registry |
   337→| Lifecycle | In-memory handle registry (`AgentHandle` with `abort()`) |
   338→| Session model | Single-query (no follow-up prompts after spawn) |
   339→
   340→**Target architecture (SandboxAgent SDK):**
   341→
   342→SandboxAgent is not limited to built-in coding agents. It is the execution layer for **any agent configured by workspace users** — coding agents, custom agents with specific tool grants, domain-specific agents with curated skills. All run through the same SandboxAgent session lifecycle with Brain's governance (dynamic MCP endpoint for tools, proxy for LLM traffic).
   343→
   344→| Component | SandboxAgent Implementation |
   345→|-----------|---------------------------|
   346→| Agent spawn | `sdk.createSession()` → agent process in sandbox (E2B, Docker, etc.) |
   347→| Agent options | SandboxAgent session config + `sdk.setMcpConfig()` + env vars |
   348→| MCP server | Brain dynamic MCP endpoint `/mcp/agent/<name>` via remote MCP config |
   349→| LLM proxy | Same — Brain proxy URL passed as `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` env |
   350→| Isolation | **Depends on provider** — see isolation section below |
   351→| Events | SandboxAgent universal event stream → event bridge → SSE registry |
   352→| Lifecycle | SandboxAgent session API (`createSession`, `prompt`, `resumeSession`, `destroySession`) |
   353→| Session model | Multi-turn (`prompt()` supports follow-ups — unblocks reject-and-retry) |
   354→| Restoration | Automatic — SDK rebinds session ID, replays events on connection loss |
   355→| Permissions | `onPermissionRequest()` → map to intent authorization (auto-approve within grants) |
   356→
   357→**Key files affected:**
   358→
   359→| File | Change |
   360→|------|--------|
   361→| `orchestrator/spawn-agent.ts` | Replace `query()` with `sdk.createSession()` + `sdk.prompt()` |
   362→| `orchestrator/agent-options.ts` | Replace SDK options with SandboxAgent session config |
   363→| `orchestrator/event-bridge.ts` | Adapt from Claude Agent SDK message format to SandboxAgent event schema |
   364→| `orchestrator/session-lifecycle.ts` | Adapt lifecycle to SandboxAgent session API; remove in-memory handle registry |
   365→| `orchestrator/worktree-manager.ts` | **Keep for local provider** — see isolation section below |
   366→| `orchestrator/routes.ts` | `POST .../prompt` becomes functional (SandboxAgent supports multi-turn) |
   367→
   368→**MCP server migration:**
   369→
   370→Currently, the Brain CLI runs as a stdio MCP server inside the agent process:
   371→```typescript
   372→// Current: stdio MCP server in agent options
   373→mcpServers: {
   374→  brain: {
   375→    type: "stdio",
   376→    command: brainCliPath,
   377→    args: ["mcp"],
   378→    env: { BRAIN_WORKSPACE_ID: workspaceId, BRAIN_IDENTITY_ID: identityId },
   379→  },
   380→}
   381→```
   382→
   383→With SandboxAgent, Brain exposes a remote MCP endpoint per agent:
   384→```typescript
   385→// Target: remote MCP server via SandboxAgent config
   386→await sdk.setMcpConfig("/workspace", "brain", {
   387→  type: "remote",
   388→  url: `${brainUrl}/mcp/agent/${agentName}`,
   389→  transport: "sse",
   390→  headers: { "X-Brain-Auth": agentToken },
   391→});
   392→```
   393→
   394→This eliminates the need to bundle the Brain CLI in the sandbox and enables per-agent tool filtering at the endpoint level.
   395→
   396→**Isolation: local provider does NOT sandbox**
   397→
   398→The local provider (`sandbox-agent` local deploy) spawns agent processes directly on the host as child processes — no containers, VMs, chroot, or kernel namespaces. Agents get full access to the host filesystem. The "worktree" in SandboxAgent is just a configurable path (`OPENCODE_COMPAT_WORKTREE`), not a git worktree.
   399→
   400→This means Brain's `worktree-manager.ts` is still needed for local deployment to provide git-level isolation per coding session:
   401→
   402→| Provider | Isolation | Brain worktree needed? |
   403→|----------|-----------|----------------------|
   404→| **Local** | None — direct host process | **Yes** — Brain creates git worktree, passes path as `cwd` to SandboxAgent session |
   405→| **Docker** | Container with bind mounts | No — mount repo into container |
   406→| **E2B** | VM (Firecracker) | No — sandboxed filesystem |
   407→| **Daytona** | Container/VM | No — sandboxed filesystem |
   408→
   409→For local deployment, Brain creates the git worktree first, then passes the worktree path as the session's `cwd`:
   410→
   411→```typescript
   412→// Local provider: Brain still manages worktrees
   413→const worktree = await createWorktree(repoPath, taskSlug);
   414→const session = await sdk.createSession({
   415→  agent: "claude",
   416→  cwd: worktree.path,  // agent operates in isolated worktree
   417→});
   418→```
   419→
   420→For cloud/container providers, the sandbox handles isolation and Brain skips worktree creation.
   421→
   422→Brain-native agents (chat, PM, observer, analytics) remain unchanged — they run in-process with AI SDK and the proxy's full tool loop.
   423→
   424→### Priority consideration
   425→
   426→SandboxAgent becomes valuable when Brain needs to run **coding agents** (Claude Code, Codex) in sandboxes for software engineering tasks. If the current focus is on chat agents and knowledge graph management (which run in Brain's own process), SandboxAgent can be deferred until the coding agent use case is prioritized.
   427→

1→# Research: Intent-to-RAR-to-MCP Tool Execution Flow
     2→
     3→**Date**: 2026-03-24 | **Researcher**: nw-researcher (Nova) | **Confidence**: High | **Sources**: 8+
     4→
     5→## Executive Summary
     6→
     7→Brain's intent-to-RAR-to-MCP tool execution flow is a 6-step pipeline that gates every external tool call behind policy evaluation, risk assessment, optional human review, and cryptographically-bound authorization tokens. The existing codebase implements 5 of the 6 steps -- the primary gap is the MCP tool proxy that validates RAR tokens against tool call parameters and forwards to upstream MCP servers.
     8→
     9→The architecture maps cleanly onto RFC 9396 (Rich Authorization Requests) and RFC 9449 (DPoP). An agent creates an intent carrying the tool name, parameters, and business justification. The intent passes through a policy gate (deterministic rule matching) and an LLM evaluator (adversarial risk scoring). Based on the risk score and policy flags, the intent is either auto-approved, sent to a human veto window, or rejected. Once authorized, the agent exchanges the intent for a DPoP-bound access token whose JWT carries `authorization_details` specifying exactly which tool, with which constraint bounds, the token permits. When the agent calls the MCP tool, the proxy verifies the token's DPoP binding (proving key possession), matches the tool name and parameters against the `authorization_details`, enforces numeric and string constraints, and only then forwards to the upstream MCP server.
    10→
    11→Three design decisions shape the extension to external MCP tools: (1) single-use vs. reusable token semantics -- high-risk tools should use single-use tokens via the `authorized -> executing` state transition, while read-only tools can use reusable tokens; (2) composite intents for multi-step tool chains -- the agent declares the full workflow upfront as multiple `BrainAction` entries; (3) tool-to-BrainAction mapping via a configurable registry rather than hardcoded route patterns. The primary implementation work is the MCP tool proxy, the MCP server registry (storing upstream endpoints and credentials), and the tool-to-action mapping layer.
    12→
    13→## Research Methodology
    14→
    15→**Search Strategy**: Codebase-first analysis of existing intent, policy, OAuth/RAR, DPoP, and MCP proxy implementations, combined with RFC 9396 and MCP protocol specifications to design the complete end-to-end flow.
    16→**Source Selection**: Types: official (IETF RFCs), technical_docs (MCP spec), codebase (primary implementation) | Reputation: high | Verification: cross-referencing RFC semantics against existing Brain types
    17→**Quality Standards**: Target 3 sources/claim (min 1 authoritative) | All major claims cross-referenced | Avg reputation: 0.9
    18→
    19→## Findings
    20→
    21→### Finding 1: Existing Intent Lifecycle and State Machine
    22→
    23→**Evidence**: The intent system implements a well-defined state machine with 8 states and deterministic transitions.
    24→
    25→**Source**: Codebase analysis - `app/src/server/intent/status-machine.ts`, `app/src/server/intent/types.ts` - Accessed 2026-03-24
    26→**Confidence**: High
    27→**Verification**: Cross-referenced `intent-queries.ts`, `intent-evaluation.ts`, `intent-routes.ts`
    28→
    29→The existing intent state machine defines the following lifecycle:
    30→
    31→```
    32→draft -> pending_auth -> pending_veto -> authorized -> executing -> completed
    33→                      -> authorized    (auto-approve)
    34→                      -> vetoed        (policy reject)
    35→                      -> failed        (eval error)
    36→         pending_veto -> authorized    (human approve / veto expires)
    37→                      -> vetoed        (human veto)
    38→         executing    -> completed
    39→                      -> failed
    40→```
    41→
    42→The `IntentRecord` already carries fields needed for RAR integration:
    43→
    44→| Field | Type | Purpose |
    45→|-------|------|---------|
    46→| `action_spec` | `{ provider, action, params? }` | Encodes the tool call identity |
    47→| `budget_limit` | `{ amount, currency }` | Financial constraint |
    48→| `authorization_details` | `BrainAction[]` | RFC 9396 authorization details |
    49→| `dpop_jwk_thumbprint` | `string` | DPoP key binding |
    50→| `token_issued_at` / `token_expires_at` | `Date` | Token lifecycle tracking |
    51→
    52→**Analysis**: The intent system is already designed to carry MCP tool call parameters through the `action_spec` field. The `authorization_details` field directly maps to RFC 9396's `authorization_details` array. The state machine transitions enforce that tokens can only be issued for intents in `authorized` status, creating a verifiable chain from intent creation to tool execution.
    53→
    54→---
    55→
    56→### Finding 2: ActionSpec-to-BrainAction Mapping for MCP Tools
    57→
    58→**Evidence**: Brain's `BrainAction` type implements RFC 9396's `authorization_details` element structure, adapted for the Brain domain.
    59→
    60→**Source**: `app/src/server/oauth/types.ts` (codebase), [RFC 9396](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9396) (IETF) - Accessed 2026-03-24
    61→**Confidence**: High
    62→**Verification**: [RFC 9396 specification](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9396.pdf), `app/src/server/oauth/rar-verifier.ts`
    63→
    64→RFC 9396 defines `authorization_details` as an array of objects with a required `type` field and optional common fields (`actions`, `locations`, `datatypes`, `identifier`, `privileges`). Brain's `BrainAction` adapts this:
    65→
    66→```typescript
    67→// RFC 9396 generic structure
    68→{
    69→  "type": "payment_initiation",          // Required: authorization type
    70→  "locations": ["https://..."],           // Optional: resource locations
    71→  "actions": ["read", "write"],           // Optional: permitted actions
    72→  "datatypes": ["contacts"],             // Optional: data types
    73→  "identifier": "account-123",           // Optional: specific resource
    74→  "privileges": ["admin"]                // Optional: privilege level
    75→}
    76→
    77→// Brain's BrainAction (domain-specific adaptation)
    78→{
    79→  "type": "brain_action",                // Fixed type for Brain domain
    80→  "action": "create",                    // Maps to RFC 9396 "actions"
    81→  "resource": "task",                    // Maps to RFC 9396 resource concept
    82→  "constraints": { "amount": 50 }        // Domain-specific constraints
    83→}
    84→```
    85→
    86→For MCP tool gating, the mapping from an intent's `action_spec` to a `BrainAction` for RAR would be:
    87→
    88→```typescript
    89→// Intent action_spec (agent-facing)
    90→{
    91→  provider: "stripe",                    // MCP server identity
    92→  action: "create_refund",              // MCP tool name
    93→  params: { amount: 50, currency: "usd", charge_id: "ch_xxx" }
    94→}
    95→
    96→// Derived BrainAction for RAR (auth-facing)
    97→{
    98→  type: "brain_action",
    99→  action: "execute",                    // Action class: execute a tool
   100→  resource: "mcp_tool:stripe:create_refund",  // Composite resource identifier
   101→  constraints: {
   102→    amount: 50,                         // Numeric bound from params
   103→    currency: "usd"                     // String identity constraint
   104→  }
   105→}
   106→```
   107→
   108→**Analysis**: The existing `ActionSpec -> BrainAction` transformation is straightforward. The `provider` + `action` fields in `action_spec` compose into a hierarchical resource identifier in the `BrainAction`. The `params` field selectively maps to `constraints` for fields that policies need to gate (e.g., financial amounts). Not all params become constraints -- only those with policy-relevant semantics.

   111→
   112→### Finding 3: Policy Evaluation Pipeline for Tool Gating
   113→
   114→**Evidence**: The policy gate evaluates intent context against workspace-scoped policies with priority-ordered rules and predicate matching.
   115→
   116→**Source**: `app/src/server/policy/policy-gate.ts`, `app/src/server/policy/types.ts` - Accessed 2026-03-24
   117→**Confidence**: High
   118→**Verification**: `app/src/server/policy/predicate-evaluator.ts`, `app/src/server/intent/authorizer.ts`
   119→
   120→The policy evaluation pipeline operates in four stages:

120→The policy evaluation pipeline operates in four stages:
   121→
   122→1. **Load**: `loadActivePolicies()` fetches all `status: "active"` policies for the workspace + identity
   123→2. **Deduplicate**: Remove duplicate policies (same ID from different query paths)
   124→3. **Evaluate**: Priority-ordered rule evaluation against `IntentEvaluationContext`
   125→4. **Gate**: First matching `deny` rule short-circuits; otherwise `allow` with veto flag
   126→
   127→Policy rules match against the `IntentEvaluationContext`, which includes:
   128→
   129→```typescript
   130→{
   131→  goal: string;                          // Natural language intent
   132→  reasoning: string;                     // Agent's justification
   133→  priority: number;                      // 0-10 priority
   134→  action_spec: {
   135→    provider: string;                    // e.g., "stripe"
   136→    action: string;                      // e.g., "create_refund"
   137→    params?: Record<string, unknown>;    // Tool parameters
   138→  };
   139→  budget_limit?: { amount, currency };
   140→  requester_type: string;                // "agent" | "human"
   141→  requester_role?: string;               // e.g., "support_agent"
   142→  behavior_scores?: Record<string, number>; // Agent behavioral track record
   143→}
   144→```
   145→
   146→For MCP tool gating, policies would use predicates like:
   147→
   148→| Rule | Condition | Effect | Use Case |
   149→|------|-----------|--------|----------|
   150→| Block large refunds | `action_spec.params.amount gt 100` | deny | Financial guardrail |
   151→| Restrict provider | `action_spec.provider not_in ["stripe", "github"]` | deny | Tool allowlist |
   152→| Auto-approve reads | `action_spec.action in ["list", "get", "search"]` | allow (no veto) | Read operations bypass veto |
   153→| Require approval for writes | `action_spec.action in ["create", "delete", "update"]` | allow + `human_veto_required` | Write operations need human approval |
   154→| Behavior-gated access | `behavior_scores.Security_First lt 0.5` | deny | Poor security behavior blocks sensitive tools |
   155→
   156→**Analysis**: The existing policy system is well-suited for MCP tool gating. The `action_spec` fields are already in the evaluation context, so policies can match on provider, action, and params without changes to the policy engine. The `human_veto_required` flag on policies naturally maps to the veto window for high-risk tool calls. The behavior score integration means agents that consistently violate security practices can be automatically restricted from sensitive tools.

   159→
   160→### Finding 4: RAR Minting via Token Endpoint (Intent-to-Token Exchange)
   161→
   162→**Evidence**: The token endpoint implements a custom OAuth grant type `urn:brain:intent-authorization` that exchanges an authorized intent for a DPoP-bound access token carrying `authorization_details`.
   163→
   164→**Source**: `app/src/server/oauth/token-endpoint.ts` (codebase), [RFC 9396 Section 3](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9396) (IETF), [RFC 9449](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9449.html) (IETF) - Accessed 2026-03-24
   165→**Confidence**: High
   166→**Verification**: `app/src/server/oauth/dpop.ts`, `app/src/server/intent/intent-queries.ts`
   167→
   168→The token exchange flow implements the following steps:
   169→
   170→```
   171→Agent                    Token Endpoint              Intent DB
   172→  |                           |                         |
   173→  |-- POST /api/auth/token -->|                         |
   174→  |   grant_type=urn:brain:intent-authorization         |
   175→  |   intent_id=<uuid>        |                         |
   176→  |   authorization_details=[BrainAction]               |
   177→  |   DPoP: <proof-jwt>       |                         |
   178→  |                           |                         |
   179→  |                           |-- getIntentById ------->|
   180→  |                           |<-- intent (authorized) -|
   181→  |                           |                         |
   182→  |                           |-- verify:               |
   183→  |                           |   1. intent.status == "authorized"
   184→  |                           |   2. DPoP thumbprint == intent.dpop_jwk_thumbprint
   185→  |                           |   3. requested auth_details match intent.authorization_details
   186→  |                           |   4. identity not revoked/suspended
   187→  |                           |                         |
   188→  |                           |-- issueAccessToken ---->|
   189→  |                           |   JWT contains:         |
   190→  |                           |   - sub: identity:xxx   |
   191→  |                           |   - cnf.jkt: thumbprint |
   192→  |                           |   - authorization_details: [BrainAction]
   193→  |                           |   - urn:brain:intent_id |
   194→  |                           |   - urn:brain:workspace |
   195→  |                           |                         |
   196→  |<-- { access_token, token_type: "DPoP", expires_in } |
   197→```
   198→
   199→The `DPoPBoundTokenClaims` type shows exactly what the issued JWT carries:
   200→
   201→```typescript
   202→{
   203→  sub: "identity:agent-uuid",
   204→  iss: "brain-as",
   205→  aud: "brain-api",
   206→  exp: 1711324800,
   207→  iat: 1711321200,
   208→  cnf: { jkt: "sha256-thumbprint-of-agent-public-key" },
   209→  authorization_details: [
   210→    { type: "brain_action", action: "execute", resource: "mcp_tool:stripe:create_refund",
   211→      constraints: { amount: 50, currency: "usd" } }
   212→  ],
   213→  "urn:brain:intent_id": "intent-uuid",
   214→  "urn:brain:workspace": "workspace-uuid",
   215→  "urn:brain:actor_type": "agent"
   216→}
   217→```
   218→
   219→Key security properties:
   220→- **DPoP binding** (RFC 9449): The `cnf.jkt` claim binds the token to the agent's key pair. Only the agent that created the intent can use the token. Stolen tokens are useless without the private key.
   221→- **Intent binding**: The `urn:brain:intent_id` claim links the token to a specific authorized intent, enabling audit trail from token usage back to intent creation, policy evaluation, and agent reasoning.
   222→- **Constraint carry-through**: The `authorization_details` in the token carry the approved constraints, which the MCP proxy validates on tool call.
   223→
   224→**Analysis**: The token endpoint already implements the full RFC 9396 + RFC 9449 integration pattern. The custom grant type `urn:brain:intent-authorization` is a clean extension point. The four-step verification (status, thumbprint, auth details match, identity check) ensures that tokens are only issued for properly authorized intents.
   225→
   226→---
   227→
   228→### Finding 5: RAR-to-Session Binding and Token Scoping
   229→
   230→**Evidence**: The DPoP-bound token is scoped to one workspace, one intent, and one set of authorized actions. Session binding is enforced at the MCP authentication middleware layer.
   231→
   232→**Source**: `app/src/server/mcp/mcp-dpop-auth.ts` (codebase), `app/src/server/oauth/dpop-middleware.ts` (codebase), [RFC 9449 Section 4](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9449.html) (IETF) - Accessed 2026-03-24
   233→**Confidence**: High
   234→**Verification**: `app/src/server/oauth/rar-verifier.ts`, `app/src/server/oauth/route-action-map.ts`
   235→
   236→The `authenticateAndAuthorize` pipeline in `mcp-dpop-auth.ts` enforces a 4-step check on every MCP request:
   237→
   238→1. **DPoP token + proof verification**: Validates the JWT signature and confirms the DPoP proof matches the token's `cnf.jkt` thumbprint. This proves the caller holds the private key.
   239→2. **Action derivation**: Maps the HTTP method + URL path to a `BrainAction` via `deriveRequestedAction()` using the route-action map.
   240→3. **Constraint extraction**: If the token's authorized actions have constraints, extracts matching fields from the request body.
   241→4. **Scope verification**: `verifyOperationScope()` checks that the requested `BrainAction` matches one of the token's `authorization_details`, including constraint bounds.
   242→
   243→The scope verification in `rar-verifier.ts` implements constraint enforcement:
   244→
   245→- **Numeric bounds**: Requested value must not exceed authorized value (e.g., `amount: 50` authorized means `amount: 30` is ok, `amount: 70` is rejected)
   246→- **String identity**: Requested value must exactly match authorized value (e.g., `currency: "usd"` must match)
   247→- **Type mismatch**: Different types treated as exceeded (fail-safe)
   248→
   249→For MCP tool calls specifically, the session binding works as follows:
   250→
   251→```
   252→Agent Session                    MCP Proxy                     Upstream MCP Server
   253→    |                               |                                |
   254→    |-- tools/call + DPoP proof --->|                                |
   255→    |                               |-- authenticateAndAuthorize:    |
   256→    |                               |   1. Verify DPoP proof        |
   257→    |                               |   2. Derive BrainAction from  |
   258→    |                               |      tool name + params       |
   259→    |                               |   3. Check authorization_details
   260→    |                               |   4. Validate constraints     |
   261→    |                               |                                |
   262→    |                               |-- Forward to upstream -------->|
   263→    |                               |<-- Tool result ----------------|
   264→    |                               |                                |
   265→    |<-- Tool result + audit -------|                                |
   266→```
   267→
   268→**Analysis**: The token is inherently session-scoped because: (a) the DPoP key pair is per-session, (b) the intent ID in the token links back to a specific agent session's trace, and (c) the `authorization_details` constrain exactly which operations the token permits. A token issued for "refund $50 on Stripe" cannot be used to "delete a GitHub issue" -- the scope verification would reject it.
   269→

   271→
   272→### Finding 6: MCP Tool Call Consumption -- The Missing Proxy Layer
   273→
   274→**Evidence**: The existing MCP route handles Brain's own MCP tools (context, decisions, tasks, etc.) but does not yet proxy to external MCP servers. The proxy layer for external tool execution is the primary gap.
   275→
   276→**Source**: `app/src/server/mcp/mcp-route.ts` (codebase), `app/src/server/proxy/` (codebase), [MCP Specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25) - Accessed 2026-03-24
   277→**Confidence**: High
   278→**Verification**: `app/src/server/proxy/anthropic-proxy-route.ts`, `docs/research/brain-native-agent-runtime.md`
   279→
   280→**Current state**: Brain's MCP route (`mcp-route.ts`) handles graph operations (context, decisions, tasks, observations, intents) directly. These are Brain-native tools. The route-action map in `route-action-map.ts` maps each MCP endpoint to a `BrainAction` for RAR verification.
   281→
   282→The LLM proxy (`proxy/anthropic-proxy-route.ts`) handles proxying to LLM providers (Anthropic, OpenRouter) with:
   283→- Identity resolution via `proxy-auth.ts`
   284→- Policy evaluation via `policy-evaluator.ts` (model access, budget, rate limits)
   285→- Cost tracking via `cost-calculator.ts`
   286→- Trace recording via `trace-writer.ts`
   287→
   288→**What's missing**: An MCP tool proxy that:
   289→1. Receives `tools/call` JSON-RPC requests from the agent
   290→2. Validates the RAR token against the requested tool + parameters
   291→3. Forwards to the upstream MCP server
   292→4. Records the tool call in the trace graph
   293→5. Returns results to the agent
   294→
   295→The MCP tool call JSON-RPC structure (per the MCP specification) is:
   296→
   297→```json
   298→{
   299→  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
   300→  "method": "tools/call",
   301→  "params": {
   302→    "name": "create_refund",
   303→    "arguments": {
   304→      "amount": 50,
   305→      "currency": "usd",
   306→      "charge_id": "ch_xxx"
   307→    }
   308→  },
   309→  "id": 1
   310→}
   311→```
   312→
   313→**Design for the MCP tool proxy**:
   314→
   315→```
   316→Agent                    Brain MCP Tool Proxy         Upstream MCP Server (e.g., Stripe)
   317→  |                           |                              |
   318→  |-- tools/call ------------>|                              |
   319→  |   + Authorization: DPoP   |                              |

320→  |   + DPoP proof            |                              |
   321→  |                           |                              |
   322→  |                           |-- 1. authenticateAndAuthorize |
   323→  |                           |   Verify DPoP + RAR scope    |
   324→  |                           |                              |
   325→  |                           |-- 2. mapToolCallToAction     |
   326→  |                           |   tool_name -> BrainAction   |
   327→  |                           |   arguments -> constraints   |
   328→  |                           |                              |
   329→  |                           |-- 3. verifyOperationScope    |
   330→  |                           |   Check constraints bounds   |
   331→  |                           |                              |
   332→  |                           |-- 4. Forward tools/call ---->|
   333→  |                           |   (original JSON-RPC)        |
   334→  |                           |                              |
   335→  |                           |<-- result -------------------|
   336→  |                           |                              |
   337→  |                           |-- 5. recordTrace             |
   338→  |                           |   tool_call trace node       |
   339→  |                           |                              |
   340→  |<-- result + trace_id -----|                              |
   341→```
   342→
   343→**Single-use vs. reusable token semantics**: The current design supports reusable tokens (the token is valid until `exp`). For MCP tools, two patterns are possible:
   344→
   345→| Pattern | When | Implementation |
   346→|---------|------|----------------|
   347→| **Single-use** | High-risk tools (financial, destructive) | Mark intent as `executing` on first use; reject subsequent calls with same intent_id |
   348→| **Reusable** | Low-risk tools (reads, searches) | Token valid until expiry; each call logged but not state-transitioned |
   349→| **Count-limited** | Batch operations | Add `max_invocations` constraint to `authorization_details`; proxy tracks count |
   350→
   351→The `authorized -> executing` transition in the status machine naturally supports single-use semantics. The proxy would call `updateIntentStatus(intentId, "executing")` on first tool call, which would prevent any subsequent token exchange for the same intent.
   352→
   353→**Analysis**: The MCP tool proxy is the key architectural piece that connects the existing intent/RAR system to external tool execution. The design reuses the existing `authenticateAndAuthorize` pipeline from `mcp-dpop-auth.ts` -- the same DPoP + RAR verification works for both Brain-native MCP tools and external MCP tool proxying. The main new work is: (a) upstream MCP server connection management, (b) tool name-to-BrainAction mapping, and (c) trace recording for external tool calls.
   354→

   356→
   357→### Finding 7: Edge Cases and Failure Modes
   358→
   359→**Evidence**: Analysis of the state machine transitions, token lifecycle, and policy evaluation reveals several edge cases that need explicit handling.
   360→
   361→**Source**: Codebase analysis, RFC 9396 Section 8 (Security Considerations), RFC 9449 Section 9 (Security Considerations) - Accessed 2026-03-24
   362→**Confidence**: Medium
   363→**Verification**: `app/src/server/intent/veto-manager.ts`, `app/src/server/intent/status-machine.ts`
   364→
   365→#### Edge Case 1: Expired RAR Token
   366→
   367→The token has an `exp` claim. If the agent takes too long between authorization and tool execution:
   368→- The DPoP middleware rejects expired tokens with `dpop_proof_expired`
   369→- The agent must create a new intent and go through the full authorization flow again
   370→- **Design choice**: Token TTL should be short (5-15 minutes) for tool calls to minimize window of exposure
   371→
   372→#### Edge Case 2: Policy Change Mid-Session
   373→
   374→If a policy is updated after an intent is authorized but before the token is used:
   375→- **Current behavior**: The token's `authorization_details` are frozen at issuance time. Policy changes do not retroactively invalidate issued tokens.
   376→- **Recommended enhancement**: Add a `policy_version` claim to the token. The proxy can optionally re-evaluate against current policies (at performance cost). For high-risk tools, re-evaluation is worth the latency.
   377→- **Existing support**: The `PolicyTraceEntry` already tracks `policy_version` on the intent's evaluation. The token could carry this for staleness detection.
   378→
   379→#### Edge Case 3: Multi-Step Tool Chains
   380→
   381→An agent workflow like "search Stripe customers -> find charge -> issue refund" requires multiple tool calls:
   382→- **Option A: Multiple intents** -- Each tool call gets its own intent. Most secure, but high friction for low-risk chains.
   383→- **Option B: Composite intent** -- One intent authorizes a sequence of actions via multiple `BrainAction` entries in `authorization_details`. The proxy checks each call against the array.
   384→- **Option C: Session-scoped token** -- Issue a token with broader `authorization_details` for the session duration. Lower security, higher ergonomics.
   385→- **Recommended**: Option B for planned chains (agent declares the full workflow upfront), Option A for ad-hoc tool calls.
   386→
   387→#### Edge Case 4: Token Refresh and Re-Authorization
   388→
   389→The current system does not support token refresh for intent-bound tokens. This is intentional:
   390→- Intent authorization includes human review (veto window). Silently refreshing would bypass this.
   391→- If the token expires, the agent creates a new intent. This forces re-evaluation, which catches policy changes.
   392→- **Exception**: For long-running operations (e.g., database migration), a separate "operation token" with longer TTL could be issued, but this requires a different grant type.
   393→
   394→#### Edge Case 5: Concurrent Intent Submission
   395→
   396→Multiple agents in the same workspace submit intents for the same tool:
   397→- Each intent is independently evaluated and authorized
   398→- Budget constraints are checked at evaluation time -- concurrent intents could exceed the budget if both pass evaluation before either deducts
   399→- **Mitigation**: The proxy's spend tracking (`spend-api.ts`) provides a near-real-time check. Combined with rate limiting, this bounds the blast radius.
   400→
   401→#### Edge Case 6: Upstream MCP Server Failure
   402→
   403→If the tool call succeeds at the proxy but fails at the upstream MCP server:
   404→- The proxy records a `failed` trace with the error
   405→- The intent transitions to `failed` (if single-use) or stays `executing` (if reusable)
   406→- The agent receives the error and can retry (creating a new intent if single-use)
   407→- **Important**: The proxy must not mark the intent as `completed` until the upstream confirms success
   408→
   409→**Analysis**: Most edge cases have clean solutions within the existing architecture. The main design decisions are around token TTL (shorter = more secure but higher friction) and multi-step chain handling (composite intents offer the best security/ergonomics tradeoff). Policy mid-session changes are the most complex edge case and warrant a `policy_version` staleness check.
   410→

input.content:
# Journey: Intent-Gated MCP Tool Call

## Actors

| Actor | Type | Interface |
|-------|------|-----------|
| Coding Agent (in sandbox) | Machine | MCP protocol (tools/list, tools/call, create_intent) |
| Human Operator | Human | Brain Feed/UI (approve/veto intents) |
| Observer Agent | Machine | Graph scan + adapter.resumeSession |
| Workspace Admin | Human | Brain Policy Management UI |

## Flow: Happy Path (Auto-Approve)

```
Coding Agent                  Brain Dynamic MCP Endpoint           Brain Intent System
     |                                    |                               |
     |-- tools/list ------------------>   |                               |
     |                                    |-- resolve session intents     |
     |                                    |-- union authorization_details |
     |                                    |-- return filtered tool list   |
     |<-- [authorized tools + gated tools with descriptions] ----------- |
     |                                    |                               |
     |-- tools/call("github:create_pr")-->|                               |
     |                                    |-- check effective scope       |
     |                                    |   tool IS in scope            |
     |                                    |-- forward to upstream MCP --->| (GitHub)
     |                                    |<-- result                     |
     |                                    |-- record trace                |
     |<-- tool result -------------------|                               |
```

## Flow: Intent Escalation (Gated Tool, Auto-Approve Policy)

```
Coding Agent                  Brain MCP Endpoint        Intent System          Policy Gate
     |                              |                        |                      |
     |-- tools/call("stripe:       |                        |                      |
     |     create_refund") -------->|                        |                      |
     |                              |-- check scope          |                      |
     |                              |   tool NOT in scope    |                      |
     |<-- 403 intent_required       |                        |                      |
     |    { tool, action_template } |                        |                      |
     |                              |                        |                      |
     |-- create_intent ------------>|                        |                      |
     |   { goal, reasoning,        |-- create + submit ---->|                      |
     |     action_spec }           |                        |-- evaluate --------->|
     |                              |                        |<-- allow, no veto   |
     |                              |                        |   auto-approve       |
     |                              |<-- authorized          |                      |
     |<-- { intent_id, status:     |                        |                      |
     |      "authorized" }          |                        |                      |
     |                              |                        |                      |
     |-- tools/call("stripe:       |                        |                      |
     |     create_refund") -------->|                        |                      |
     |                              |-- check scope (now     |                      |
     |                              |   includes new intent) |                      |
     |                              |   tool IS in scope     |                      |
     |                              |-- forward to Stripe -->|                      |
     |<-- refund result ------------|                        |                      |
```

## Flow: Intent Escalation with Human Veto (Yield-and-Resume)

```
Coding Agent        Brain MCP          Intent System     Human Operator    Observer
     |                  |                    |                 |               |
     |-- tools/call --->|                    |                 |               |
     |<-- 403           |                    |                 |               |
     |                  |                    |                 |               |
     |-- create_intent->|-- create+submit -->|                 |               |
     |                  |                    |-- evaluate       |               |
     |                  |                    |   policy: veto   |               |
     |                  |                    |   required       |               |
     |                  |<-- pending_veto    |                 |               |
     |<-- { status:     |                    |                 |               |
     |   "pending_veto"}|                    |                 |               |
     |                  |                    |                 |               |
     | [AGENT YIELDS -- session goes idle]   |                 |               |
     |                  |                    |                 |               |
     |                  |                    |-- surfaces in -->|               |
     |                  |                    |   governance     |               |
     |                  |                    |   feed           |               |
     |                  |                    |                 |               |
     |                  |                    |<-- approve ------|               |
     |                  |                    |   authorized     |               |
     |                  |                    |                 |               |
     |                  |                    |                 |    [graph scan]
     |                  |                    |                 |               |
     |                  |                    |                 |  detects auth |
     |                  |                    |                 |  intent for   |
     |                  |                    |                 |  idle session |
     |                  |                    |                 |               |
     | [OBSERVER TRIGGERS RESUME] <------------------------------------|
     |                  |                    |                 |               |
     |-- (session       |                    |                 |               |
     |    resumes)      |                    |                 |               |
     |                  |                    |                 |               |
     |-- tools/call --->|-- scope check      |                 |               |
     |                  |   (now in scope)   |                 |               |
     |                  |-- forward -------->|                 |               |
     |<-- result -------|                    |                 |               |
```

## Flow: Rejected Intent

```
Coding Agent        Brain MCP          Intent System     Policy Gate
     |                  |                    |                 |
     |-- tools/call --->|                    |                 |
     |<-- 403           |                    |                 |
     |                  |                    |                 |
     |-- create_intent->|-- create+submit -->|                 |
     |                  |                    |-- evaluate ----->|
     |                  |                    |<-- deny          |
     |                  |                    |   (policy block) |
     |                  |<-- vetoed          |                 |
     |<-- { status:     |                    |                 |
     |   "vetoed",      |                    |                 |
     |   reason: "..." }|                    |                 |
     |                  |                    |                 |
     | [Agent adapts -- tries alternative approach or reports to user]
```

## Shared Artifacts

| Artifact | Source | Consumers |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| `proxy_token` | Session spawn (R2) | MCP endpoint auth, tools/list, tools/call |
| `agent_session` record | Session store | MCP endpoint (session lookup), observer (idle detection) |
| `intent` record | Intent system | Policy gate, MCP scope check, observer resume |
| `gates` edge (session->intent) | Intent creation | tools/list scope union, tools/call scope check |
| `authorization_details` | Intent + policy evaluation | tools/list filtering, tools/call validation |
| `action_spec_template` | 403 response | Agent's create_intent call |
| `mcp_tool` registry | Tool registry | tools/list, 403 error templates |

## Error Paths

| Error | Trigger | Agent Sees | Recovery |
|-------|---------|-----------|----------|
| Tool not in scope | tools/call for ungated tool | 403 `intent_required` + template | Create intent |
| Intent vetoed | Policy denies or human vetoes | `vetoed` status + reason | Try alternative or report |
| Intent timeout | Veto window expires without human action | `authorized` (auto-approve on expiry) | Automatic |
| Session not found | Invalid proxy token | 401 | Re-spawn session |
| Upstream MCP failure | Tool server down | 502 with error detail | Retry or skip |
| Constraint exceeded | Intent amount < actual | 403 constraint violation | Create new intent with correct params |
| Concurrent scope change | Policy updated mid-session | Next tools/call may fail | Create new intent |

## Integration Checkpoints

1. **Proxy token -> session -> intents**: Token resolves to session; session's `gates` edges resolve to intents; intents' `authorization_details` form effective scope
2. **tools/list consistency**: Listed tools must match what tools/call will accept (no lies in the listing)
3. **Intent -> policy -> scope**: Intent authorization must update the effective scope visible to subsequent tools/list and tools/call
4. **Observer -> session resume**: Observer must detect authorized intents for idle sessions and trigger adapter.resumeSession reliably
5. **Trace completeness**: Every tools/call (success or failure) must produce a trace record linked to session and intent
